file_name: Matveeva - 2025 - Multimodal Neural Network Analysis of Raman Spectra and Dermoscopic Images of Skin Tumors.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used convolutional neural networks (CNNs) for the classification of skin tumors (malignant melanoma vs. benign pigmented neoplasms) based on multimodal joint analysis of Raman spectra and dermatoscopic images. The study reports ROC AUC metrics (0.93 for the multimodal model) obtained via this deep learning approach.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the collection of in vivo Raman spectra and dermatoscopic images from human subjects, the development of a multimodal convolutional neural network (CNN) model, and the reporting of new diagnostic accuracy results (ROC AUC of 0.93) for skin tumor classification. It includes sections on experimental data, methods, results, and conclusions based on primary data analysis, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Multimodal Neural Network Analysis of Raman Spectra and Dermoscopic Images of Skin Tumors
journal: Bulletin of the Russian Academy of Sciences: Physics
brief_summary: This study developed a multimodal convolutional neural network to classify malignant melanoma versus benign pigmented neoplasms using joint analysis of in vivo Raman spectra and dermatoscopic images. The dataset comprised 65 malignant melanomas, 111 seborrheic keratoses, and 167 pigmented nevi from human subjects, supplemented by HAM10K images for dermoscopic training. The model architecture utilized a modified CNN for spectral data and a reduced VGG16 for images, with classification performed via logistic regression of predicted values. Evaluation using 10-fold cross-validation demonstrated that the multimodal approach achieved a ROC AUC of 0.93, outperforming single-modality models which yielded AUCs of 0.90 and 0.86 respectively. The results indicate that combining spectral and spatial features significantly improves diagnostic accuracy compared to using either modality alone.

lesion_types_studied.details: Used as the positive class in binary classification against benign pigmented neoplasms. Dataset contained 65 malignant melanomas from human subjects and 1113 images from HAM10K.
Grouped with pigmented nevi as 'benign pigmented neoplasms' for binary classification. Dataset contained 111 seborrheic keratoses from human subjects and 1099 images from HAM10K.
Grouped with seborrheic keratoses as 'benign pigmented neoplasms' for binary classification. Dataset contained 167 pigmented nevi from human subjects and 6705 images from HAM10K.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic category: malignant melanoma vs. benign pigmented neoplasms (seborrheic keratoses and pigmented nevi)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the development of a 'classification model for malignant melanoma and benign pigmented neoplasms'. The task involves distinguishing between two discrete diagnostic categories (malignant vs. benign). Evaluation metrics include ROC AUC (0.93 for the multimodal model, 0.90 for Raman, 0.86 for dermoscopy), which is standard for binary classification tasks. The output involves logistic regression of predicted values to assign class labels.

datasets_used.name: In vivo Raman and Dermoscopic Dataset (Samara)
datasets_used.description: A proprietary dataset of in vivo skin Raman spectra and corresponding dermatoscopic images collected from human subjects using a two-channel experimental system (portable Raman setup and multispectral digital dermatoscope) at Samara State Medical University. Histological analysis was used to establish diagnoses.

use.training: Used for training the Raman spectra CNN and the dermoscopic CNN (after transfer learning on HAM10K). The data was split into training, validation, and test samples in a ratio of 8:1:1.
use.validation: Used for validation (part of the 8:1:1 split).
use.testing: Used for testing (part of the 8:1:1 split). Stability tested by 10-fold cross-validation.
use.other: Used for final evaluation of the multimodal model via logistic regression of predicted values.

datasets_used.name: HAM10K (Human Against Machine with 10000 training images)
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic image dataset used to supplement the small local dataset of dermatoscopic images to avoid overfitting and instability in the VGG16 architecture.

use.training: Used for pre-training the dermoscopic CNN via transfer learning. The network was trained on HAM10K data (8917 images) before being further trained on the local dataset.
use.other: Used for pretraining/transfer learning to initialize weights for the dermoscopic image analysis branch.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To test the stability of the obtained models and the resulting combined multimodal model.
To assess classification quality and compare the diagnostic performance of models based on Raman spectra, dermoscopic images, and their joint analysis.
To quantify the diagnostic accuracy of the classification models (ROC AUC values of 0.90, 0.86, and 0.93 were reported for Raman, dermoscopy, and multimodal models, respectively).
To perform the final classification by combining predicted model values from the analysis of Raman spectra and dermoscopic images.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). Model performance was evaluated using ROC AUC metrics and 10-fold cross-validation for stability, but no statistical significance testing between models was reported.

In vivo Raman and Dermoscopic Dataset (S.notes: The multimodal model's ROC AUC (0.93) exceeded the single-modality models (Raman: 0.90, Dermoscopic: 0.86) by 3% and 7% respectively. The results were validated using 10-fold cross-validation. The study compared these CNN results to a previous PLS-DA study [6] on the same dataset which yielded ROC AUCs between 0.60 and 0.81, noting the current CNN results were 6% higher.

HAM10K.notes: HAM10K was used to pre-train the dermoscopic CNN to avoid overfitting due to the small size of the local dermatoscopic dataset. The network was trained on 8917 HAM10K images before being fine-tuned on the local dataset. No specific performance metrics (like AUC) are reported for the model trained solely on HAM10K in this context; it served as a feature extractor/initializer for the final multimodal model evaluated on the Samara dataset.

architecture_used.main_model: Multimodal Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) consisting of two branches: a modified CNN for Raman spectra and a reduced VGG16 for dermoscopic images, combined via logistic regression.
architecture_used.pretraining: The dermoscopic image branch (reduced VGG16) was pre-trained on the HAM10K dataset (8917 images) using transfer learning. The four upper layers were frozen during fine-tuning on the local dataset.

input_size.raman_spectra: Pre-processed spectral samples (cropped to 860–920 nm range)

raman_branch.layers: Convolutional layers (Conv 1-1 to Conv 4-1), Pooling layers, Flatten layer, Dense layers, ReLU activation functions

dermoscopic_branch.reason_for_reduction: To avoid overfitting and instability due to small sample size of local dermatoscopic images
dermoscopic_branch.layers: Convolutional layers (Conv 1-1 to Conv 5-1), Pooling layers, Dropout layer, Flatten layer, Dense layers, ReLU activation functions

training_details.data_split: 8:1:1 ratio for training, validation, and test samples
training_details.hardware: Intel(R) Core(TM) i7-10700KF CPU @ 3.80GHz, 32 GB RAM, GeForce RTX 3080 graphics card

classification_or_output_head.input: Predicted model values from the analysis of Raman spectra and dermoscopic images
classification_or_output_head.output: Binary classification (Malignant melanoma vs. Benign pigmented neoplasms)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for Raman spectra analysis
Modified CNN for Raman spectra analysis
Reduced VGG16 architecture for dermoscopic images
PLS-DA (Partial Least Squares Discriminant Analysis) - mentioned as a baseline/comparison from previous study [6]
preprocessing_techniques: Raman spectra cropping to the range of 860–920 nm (corresponding to 1114–1874 cm⁻¹)
Raman spectra smoothing with a Savitzky–Golay filter
Dermatoscopic image registration in polarized illumination mode
Data augmentation for both Raman spectra and dermatoscopic image datasets

data_augmentation_techniques.details: The paper states that 'Both data sets were augmented' (referring to the local dataset of 338 images and the HAM10K dataset of 8917 images used for transfer learning). However, specific details such as the types of transforms (e.g., rotation, flipping), probabilities, or ranges are not reported in the provided text.

conclusions: The proposed multimodal joint analysis of Raman spectra and dermatoscopic images for binary classification of malignant melanoma versus benign pigmented neoplasms achieved a ROC AUC of 0.93.

The multimodal model outperformed single-modality models, exceeding the accuracy of models based solely on Raman spectra (ROC AUC = 0.90) by 3% and solely on dermatoscopic images (ROC AUC = 0.86) by 7%.
The improvement in diagnostic accuracy is attributed to the complementarity of spatial features from dermoscopy and spectral features from Raman spectroscopy.
The CNN-based multimodal approach yielded results (ROC AUC 0.86–0.93) that were 6% higher than previous PLS-DA analysis of Raman spectra on the same dataset (ROC AUC 0.60–0.81).
The multimodal method identified skin neoplasms more effectively on average than medical specialists, who achieved a ROC AUC of 0.82 in a comparative study.
The study confirms the potential for using this multimodal method in clinical practice for automated screening diagnostics to detect skin neoplasms at an early stage.
important_remarks: The study relies on a small, single-center dataset (343 total lesions) collected from Samara State Medical University, limiting generalizability and raising concerns about population bias.

The dermoscopic image branch utilized transfer learning from the HAM10K dataset (8917 images) to mitigate overfitting due to the small local dataset size (338 images), which introduces potential domain shift issues between the pre-training and target domains.

No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was performed to compare the multimodal model against single-modality models or human experts; performance differences are reported only as absolute ROC AUC values.

The comparison to human dermatologists (ROC AUC 0.82) is based on a cited external study (Haenssle et al.) rather than a concurrent head-to-head trial, making direct performance equivalence claims speculative.

The study compares CNN results to a previous PLS-DA study on the same dataset, noting a 6% improvement, but does not provide a rigorous statistical basis for this improvement beyond metric comparison.

Data leakage risks are mitigated by using 10-fold cross-validation and an 8:1:1 train/validation/test split, but the small sample size (65 MM, 111 SK, 167 NE) makes the model sensitive to specific sample selection within folds.

The multimodal model architecture combines outputs via logistic regression, which is a simple linear combination method; the paper does not explore more complex fusion techniques (e.g., attention mechanisms) that might better capture inter-modal dependencies.
The hardware used for training (Intel i7-10700KF, RTX 3080) is standard consumer-grade, but the computational cost and inference speed required for clinical deployment are not discussed.

The study focuses only on binary classification (malignant melanoma vs. benign pigmented neoplasms), excluding other skin cancer types (e.g., basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma), limiting its clinical scope.
